speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number five, July 12th, 1995. Interview with Izchak Goldblatt.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I'm quite tired already. I think this will be the finishing-- the finishing part of it, because there's just so much I can take of this. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm born-- I was born on the 5th of May. The 5th of May, for me, is a very, very special-- special date, because a few things happened on the 5th of May. I was liberated on the 5th of May, 1945. But the significance of the liberation was that, before that, I had a dream I spoke to my father. But I have to give you a little bit of something that happened on the 2nd of May. On the 2nd of May, somebody let out a rumor that the Americans are here. And all of us in our block started jumping down from our cots and jump in the middle of block. [INAUDIBLE]. Americans are here. We  are free. After about 10, 15 minutes of such-- such happiness, the kapos came in and started beating us up, and out to work. And I don't know-- for some reason we were late to work. And that's probably why somebody made that. The disappointment-- to describe to you the disappointment [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of what happened then, I cannot-- I am not capable of describing. I don't have words to describe to you what kind of disappointment that was, that the-- that after we thought we are free, that we have to go back to work and everything like normal. I have a friend-- I had a friend who killed himself that day. That night, when we came back, he covered himself with a blanket and he said he doesn't want to live. He strangled himself. [INAUDIBLE] from Sieghart. I knew him very well. He-- and he was laying in the next cot to me, and we couldn't talk him out of it. The disappointment, this is what did it to him. So that's the only example I can give you what disappointment-- such a disappointment can do. Now, on the night-- on the 4th of May, I have a dream. I dream with my father, and we were speaking. And I said in the dream, Dad, I think that we will be liberated on the fifth day of the fifth month and the fifth hour. And he said, no, it will be the fifth day, the fifth month, but double that-- on the 10th hour. And sure enough, 10:00 on the 5th of May, the first American soldier came into the camp, and we were liberated. So that date has a particular significance-- significance for me. When the Americans liberated us, of course, we were so hungry that we-- we went out into the neighborhood fields, and into the little town of Ebensee. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And I will tell you something. We were so weak, that we didn't speak any languages. I read and write seven languages. At that time, I read and wrote-- I read and wrote six languages. Italian I learned after the war. But when I was liberated through the Americans, I spoke only one language, the language of the hungry. I knew Herr Meister, Brot, soup, I knew those words-- Arbeit. I knew the words that were related to the camp. And I had a friend who was in the hospital. He was hospitalized. And at the hospital, they didn't feed them. So his brother said, let's go out into Ebensee, to the city and bring some fruit for Icu. He's also Icu-- was also Icu, his name. He's not alive anymore, unfortunately. Icu Unger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went out, and at the-- at the beginning of the city of Ebensee stands an American soldier with a rifle in hand and says, go back. Go back to camp. And he starts-- he tries to talk to us. I don't understand one word what he is saying. I tell you, like the others-- like the others that were there-- we were, like, 10, 12 people there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't understand anything he is saying. And then he goes on and says, go back. They brought bread. They brought meat. He said the word meat-- like, a window snapped open in my brain, and suddenly I remembered all my English that I knew before.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I start speaking to the guy. Where is he from, how are you, and what is going on, have they really brought food to the camp? He said, yes, they have brought food. You can go back to camp. They will give it to you slowly, slowly. So suddenly, I was a-- nice. But we say, I told him, listen, we have a friend who is at the hospital. They don't feed them. I have to bring this friend of mine-- I have to bring him some food. Let me go in. So we go in, and the very nice, very intriguing episode, I must tell you-- just by the way. On the way into the city, we meet two nuns who tell us, go there in the other street. There is a store that some people have broken open. It's deserted. Nobody is there. And you can take food from there, whatever you want.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so I don't want to say that I was the one, but one of us said-- made like this. Yeah, but somebody will see us. Meaning, we showed upwards. God will see us. Because no people are there or no police is there, we are not going to take. This was just to tell you how religious we were and how moral we were, that even after suffering so much hunger, we weren't going to rob a business. We said, somebody will see us. God will see us. And we went into the city. We went into some houses, and we begged for bread, for some things. They gave us, I will tell you, bread and butter, and what we called [NON-ENGLISH] which is fat off-- I don't know if it was pork fat or chicken fat or whatever, that frozen fat. And sugar and jam, all in one. And we ate it, because we were so hungry. We ate with our eyes. And on the way back, we see a soldier pouring out some noodles into a ditch. So we go-- we go into the ditch, we fill our pockets with noodles, and we eat the noodles from-- We were not hungry, because we ate in the city. We ate this fat and all kinds of things. But just to give you a little bit of understanding how we felt. Now, after that, we all get diarrhea, of course. All get diarrhea, and I say, I'm not going-- until I have diarrhea, I can't serve myself. I can't go down from the bed. I'm not going to the hospital. So my friends took care of me in the barracks. The third day, I felt better. I said, now I can go to the hospital. Maybe they will give me some medication.

speaker: interviewer
text: I go to the hospital, and one nurse brings me a bedpan. And I say, thank you very much. She said, do you speak English? I say, yes. She says, Dr. Marshall! She starts screaming. Dr. Marshall, I have someone who speaks English. What happened? They were looking for somebody speaking English with-- with candles, because they found a lot of medication, and they couldn't read the labels. They didn't know what was vitamins, what was medication, what was what for. They needed somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, in the hospital, the huge hospital tent, the field hospital, there were, like, 250 or 300 sick people. Now, some speak Yiddish, some Romanian, some German, some Hungarian, some English. No, there was one who spoke English in there, but he was so-- so bad, he couldn't even walk down. He couldn't go down from his bed. And they couldn't-- the doctor didn't understand what is hurting them. He goes visiting them. He puts a stethoscope to their chest, but he doesn't know [INAUDIBLE]. He doesn't know what is wrong with them. Now they have a treasure. Immediately, a captain comes, Dr. Marshall. And he brings me a piece of chocolate. And he says, the chocolate will calm your diarrhea. And-- and-- and he says, tomorrow you will feel better, and you will come with me and visit. And sure enough, tomorrow morning, I go around from bed to bed and tell him each one, what hurts him. And the medications that they showed me, and I translated for them. It was-- it was such a great thing. And at the end of the tent, there lays a friend of mine, who studied English with me. And he says, Icu, I don't understand one word that you are talking to the doctor. I knew as much English as you do. I said, [INAUDIBLE], don't worry. And I told him the story with the American soldier, that somebody will say one wood, and suddenly it  will click open. He lives in Haifa, Bati Rosenberg. He's well, and he speaks English very well. [LAUGHS] I think I would end it with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's just talk a little bit about where you went after the war and a little bit about your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my troubles seemed not to have ended. After the war, I heard-- we went from Ebensee, immediately after the war. The next day, we went out to [INAUDIBLE] Budapest, to the Hungarian capital. In Budapest, I meet a boy. He says that two of my brothers are alive, and they are in Arad, in a certain city in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I go to Arad. I go to Arad. I meet my bro-- I meet my brother. There is a picture. I came. I was dressed in American battle dress, and American pants, working pants of the American army, and the cap like that. And I have a picture taken in Arad.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was so thin, so skinny, that my brother, every day, fed me whatever he could push into me. And he told me that the other brother went into another city, Nagyvarad, which is not far away from there. And then I moved also to Nagyvarad. In Nagyvarad, we heard that three of our sisters are alive, and they came also to Nagyvarad. Oradea [INAUDIBLE] it's called now. It's Romania. Then it was Hungarian, before the war. Now  it was Romanian. It was given back to the Romanians. And we met our sisters. And a nice little funny episode-- I got so fat. I was always a skinny boy at home. And it was like 2:00, 3:00 in the morning when they arrived from the train, and we brought them home to some relative. And I helped bringing in the valises, their-- their baggages. And my oldest sister said-- said to one of the other sisters, [YIDDISH]. Give something to the [YIDDISH]. I helped them bring in the baggage. And only when I smiled, she recognized me. Oh, this is Icu. She didn't know that-- she didn't recognize me, because I was suddenly round in the face, so blown up. Another little curiosity of what eating can do to you and not eating can do to you. And then, after six months, we decided we will go to Israel. And going to Israel means that you walk all the borders, the Romania-Hungarian border, the whole night. The Hungarian-- Austria border-- Austrian border, the whole night. And then Austria was divided in three zones-- the British, English-- British, American, and French zone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you cross-- each one has a border. You have to cross by night. And we got to the American zone, where we heard that there is an organization of Aliyah Bet that will take us to Israel. We were in a camp called Admont, which is close to a city called Graz, not far away from Klagenfurt. We were there for six months, doing nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was-- I mean, all-- most of the people were doing nothing. I was working in the-- in the store where we provided the food for the people. And since I spoke English, they needed translation, so they took me to the office to translate when they needed that. I knew German and I knew English, and they caught SS people running away, or SS people who were hiding, like, DP persons. But people recognized them or found out that they are not one of ours, so they had to question them. So I was there. And then, after-- after Admont, we went to Italy. In 31st-- the night of 31st of December, 1945, we-- my brother, one brother of mine, and myself and my sister-- one of my sisters-- the three of us crossed the border, the Brenners. Now, the Brenners if you know-- now I know very well-- that if I want to go to Genoa, in Switzerland, from Milano, I have to take the car, put the car on the train, and cross the Brenner Mountains in the tunnel, with the train, because the Brenners, even in May, June, is covered with snow. We walked on the 31st of December, 1945. We crossed the border into Italy, from Austria into Italy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Probably the border guards were paid off by the-- by the Israeli troops, by the Haganah that they-- we were crossing, like, a few hundred meters behind them. Or maybe they didn't hear it in the snow. And we were there. We came to a certain-- to a certain man who had a shack and kept us in that shack. We were 101 persons. He kept us in a shack for-- it was a Saturday night and the whole of Sunday. Was-- that was New Year's, the 1st of January. The whole of Sunday we were in that shack. And I thought that after Auschwitz, Wolfsberg, Ebensee, here is where I'm going to die. I'm going to freeze to death. This is unbelievable, the cold it was. Up in the mountains on the Brenner, it was so bitter cold, you can't imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then my brother went into this farmer whose shack it was, and he gave him two or three cigarettes that he had. And the farmer let us in into the-- how do you call the place where the cows are? Like the barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: The barn, where the cows are, in a barn? He allowed us to go into the barn with the cows, and we laid on the cows to warm up. And that saved our lives, because we had to wait till, like, 10:00 in the evening. If we had to stay out there till 10 o'clock, I don't believe we would have survived it. And then-- so we warmed up, and he gave us some hot water to drink instead of tea. Hot water, just water. He didn't have tea. And at 10:00 came the trucks and took us towards-- towards Milano, towards Milano, into Italy. And as we are driving, sure enough one of the trucks, the driver fell asleep, goes into a ditch, truck turns around. And they take out all the women. They say, go into another truck. And the men, go hide into the field until the morning when they will come-- another truck will come and pick us up. We went-- I can tell you, this was wintertime. The sun was shining beautifully. The fields were frozen on the top only. And when you stepped in, you walked into water and mud till, like, almost your knees. And it was cold, of course, and you had to hide in the forest so that the Italians-- the Italian [INAUDIBLE]-- don't catch us and don't put us over the border again, so we don't have to redo it next week again, the same crossing the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we hid there until another truck came. It was almost evening by that time. They said they would come, like, lunchtime. He came almost in the evening. So we were a whole day in the forest. And they took us into Milano.

speaker: interviewee
text: There, of course, they fed us and put us to bed. They gave us sheets. This was the first time, I think, after the war that I really slept in sheets, in white sheets, white linen. Even at home, after the war, we slept just like this, because everything was temporary, everything was-- just-- we were just going to go back, going towards Israel. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were in Milano. In Milano, we were only for a few days. They took us near Rome into a place called La [INAUDIBLE]. There was a group of about 100 or something people. And we stayed in a beautiful villa in a very nice resort place, about 20, 25 kilometers from Rome, near the sea. Beautiful, beautiful-- I was playing ping pong day and night. I taught-- the doctor who was living in there, it was a Jewish woman. I taught her English, and the nurse. And whoever wanted English, I taught them English. And we were playing soccer. We-- we had a meshuggah life. We had it very good. But, you know, you live 20-- 20 men in one room and 20 women in another room, and another 20 in a different room. You know, the whole-- it was living like-- not in great comfort, but for us, that was already comfort. We stayed there for about five months. Then they took us to Bari, south, to somewhere in Italy, where we were staying in a camp near Bari for about three, four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they took us one night and embarked us on a boat-- on a boat which, I don't know the size of it, [INAUDIBLE] but it must have been small. Why do I know it was small? Because after four days, we arrived-- [INAUDIBLE] we were on the shores of Tel Aviv. We saw Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the British came with two battleships and squeezed our boat and jumped into-- and we were throwing cans of food. We were-- we were-- the Haganah, the policy was that we were to show that we fight them. We were to-- to set-- to put up a fight against the British, not to go like we went to Auschwitz. That won't happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we fought with them with sticks and with cans and with whatever we could. But then the boat started cracking, and-- so they jumped the boat, they came, they settled down. And when-- as we-- as we go in the boat, I hear two soldiers behind me speak. Tom, have you seen-- they put probably, like, 450 people in such a small boat. How crowded can they be? But I knew that we were 1,257 people on that boat. How did I know? My brother-in-law was the secretary of the-- of the ship, of the vessel. And he knew every person.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sleeping three floors, one over the other. No separation between men and women, one near the other, in our dresses. You couldn't undress. When you had to go out, it was a big story to go out-- walk out from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one Friday night, the ship, near Greece, was turning in a turmoil-- in a turm-- what do you call it? In a whirlpool, like, of-- we thought we would be drowning, and it creaked. And we were crying, [YIDDISH]. We were praying to God that he help us out from that water, that we don't sink.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- there were two ships who sank in that spot, they told us later. So-- but we got out of it, and we came to Tel Aviv. And they dragged the ship to Haifa, the British, and put us in a-- they had these prison boats, where you were-- couldn't jump off the boat. They were high fenced in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they put us into the boat and into a ship and took us to Cyprus, where they kept us. We were-- in the summer camps, we were, like, 10,000 people. In the winter camps-- this was near [INAUDIBLE]. There were winter camps near-- near-- near what's called the capital? Nicosia, the capital of Cyprus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Nicosia, they had, like, 50,000 Jews there. They didn't want to let them come to Israel. So in Cyprus, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were in these summer camps. And I can't remember the first time that I met this heat, because we weren't used to such kind of heat. We were standing in line that they put DDT in our clothes. They were the-- to delouse us. It was so bitter hot. So many people fell down, fainted. And then, they divided us into tents. Every group-- you belonged to a certain kibbutz, kind of. You were organized in kibbutzim. So every group belonged to a certain kibbutz, and we went and  we got a few tents that belonged to us. The other group, the other party, got another group of tents. And I remember we would stand up, like, 2:00 in the night. There was one-- there wasn't a well. There was one tube coming out from the Earth that was flowing water. That was the only water in the camp until we built-- they made a little bit more sophisticated water. You couldn't shower. And we were there-- it was very, very miserable at the beginning. It was very, very bad. And we were fenced in by the British with watchtowers just like the Germans. Except that they wouldn't throw stones. They wouldn't kill you. But they were just as bad. They were bad enough-- bad enough, I would say. Inside the camp, we had our freedom. We would play soccer. We would play ping pong a lot. We would-- we had a school. My brother-in-law was the director of the school. I taught-- I gave a Hebrew lecture. Every morning I had a class. I was the youngest in my class. I was the teacher. And in the afternoon, I gave-- I-- I taught English. All the teachers of the school, I gave them an English lesson every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Relatively, a few weeks later when we had already our own showers-- you know, a shower you would set up a pipe. On top of it, you would make a barrel, drill a few holes. It has a thing that you can close the water-- how do you call it? A tab that you can close or open. That was the shower. That was a shower. But it was already good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Other than that, we had quite a good time, relatively a good time. We-- they taught us how to-- how to dismantle weapons. You know, pre-military training, because they needed us, if we go to Israel, that we be ready to be soldiers. And sure enough, many of our friends went, and they were not well trained. All we knew was [INAUDIBLE], how to dismantle, and the-- and the [INAUDIBLE]. But we didn't even know how to put on our [INAUDIBLE], you know, that army rucksack and the bullets and everything. We didn't know how to put that on, because that we never had in Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got to Israel-- after 20 months and one day in Cyprus, we closed the camp. And I took the ping pong table with us, and we came to Israel. And we took it to the camp, straight to the army camp. And I settle-- I set up a ping pong tournament there in the camp. And-- and one-- one guy from Czechoslovakia was the first, and I was the second, and my brother was the third. And we all got four days' leave because we won a surprise for winning the tournament. There were many people playing ping pong, but we were the best. So we got the prize of four days' leave. And when we come back, all our friends were gone. They became the personal bodyguards for the president in [INAUDIBLE]. They had a fantastic job. They were doing their six or seven hours of watching around the premises of Dr. [? Weizman ?]. And the rest, during the day, they would work, they would build, they would make some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where are we going? So-- and now they split us. There was a law that two brothers cannot be in the same unit. So they sent my brother to one place, and me they sent to another place. And I was crying. What are you doing? I've always been with my brother since I'm liberated. Isn't-- wasn't that enough that I had to be in Auschwitz? Now let me go with my brother. So the sergeant says to me, you see these three stripes? I give you the three stripes if you let me go where you-- in your place, where you are sent to go. Where am I sent to go? Headquarters. Headquarters, or something, in the city, in Ramat Gan. It still is in Ramat Gan, but a different location. They moved since.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would eat at home and sleep at home. I had a sister there. One of the sisters settled down in Jaffa, so I would go home to sleep, and I would come in the morning to work. Just-- not like a soldier. I wouldn't carry a weapon. I never shot. I never shot a shot. Although--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live in Israel for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in Israel from January 31st, 1949 till 30th of June, 1980. I mean, off and on. I went around, went all over the world. I went to the Far East, et cetera, et cetera. But I was an Israeli-- Israeli citizen till then. And since then, I'm living in the United States. And now I am a American-- an American citizen. And we'll go back to Israel soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your wife are moving back?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are going back to Israel because our children are there, except for the one son. We have-- I have two daughters and-- three daughters and seven grandchildren. So we are going back to Israel now, on the 23rd of July, God willing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is my story, more or less, if I haven't forgotten anything, which I probably did.
